metadata.title: Baroness Stowell Chair, Digital and Communications Committee House of Lords Houses of Parliament SW1
metadata.author: download (4)

chunks.text: Baroness Stowell Chair, Digital and Communications Committee House of Lords Houses of Parliament SW1A 0AA Dear Chair, st January 2023 I hope this correspondence finds you well. I am writing to you in my capacity as SVP and Head of Global Government Relations and Policy at Match Group, the parent company of over 45 online dating apps including Tinder, Hinge and Match. Match Group is a firm advocate of the UK’s Online Safety Bill and of the general need for companies and regulators todomore to advance online safety efforts. We sincerely thank you for your commitment to pass a strong, robust Online Safety Bill. Match Group has been a ctively engaged in supporting the progression of this legislation. Accordingly, we have previously submitted evidence to your Committee and followed the current inquiry with sincere interest, including the evidence session on Wednesday 25 th January when the Committee heard from Orla MacRae, Deputy Director of Online Harms Regulation at the Department for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport (DCMS). I am writing to you today to note Match Group’s deep concern regarding Ms. MacRae’s assertion that as app stores arenotuser-to-user services or search engines, that app stores do not have “the largest overall control over what children access online”. Match Group can categorically state that this statement is false. App users directly interact with both developers – such as Match – and the app distribution layer – the Apple App Store and the Google Play Store – andbothmustact to protect our children. App stores are not simply innate digital “infrastructure”, as Ms. MacRae suggested, with no input on the content of services. Apple and Google have a direct monopoly of access and control over digital content, yet no responsibility to protect those who use their services every day. We have engaged extensively with public consultations in support of the Bill, including those from your Committee, the Digital, Culture, Media and Sport Select Committee, Ofcom, the CMA, and DCMS, highlighting the role of the distribution layer in the app ecosystem. We were highly surprised to therefore hear a senior official misunderstand the fundamental workings of our industry. Match Group’s views on this matter are directly in for med by the success of our portfolio brands operating in the mobile app ecosystem. By choice, Match Group’s portfolio brands only allow individuals 18 years of age or older to use their services. We take great strides to keep underage users from using our platform with the limited user data provided by the app stores. Our day-to-day experiences operating in the app ecosystem compel us to call for a legislative duty to be placed upon app stores to ensure that they are doing their part to protect children. Match Group strongly supports the current language in the bill that subjects developers tonewst and ards to protect children. We donotseek to lessen or diminish the force of that language.

We sincerely thank you for your commitment to pass a strong, robust Online Safety Bill. Match Group has been a ctively engaged in supporting the progression of this legislation. We have engaged extensively with public consultations in support of the Bill, including those from your Committee, the Digital, Culture, Media and Sport Select Committee, Ofcom, the CMA, and DCMS, highlighting the role of the distribution layer in the app ecosystem. We were highly surprised to therefore hear a senior official misunderstand the fundamental workings of our industry. Match Group’s views on this matter are directly in for med by the success of our portfolio brands operating in the mobile app ecosystem. By choice, Match Group’s portfolio brands only allow individuals 18 years of age or older to use their services. We take great strides to keep underage users from using our platform with the limited user data provided by the app stores. chunks.text: a legislative duty to be placed upon app stores to ensure that they are doing their part to protect children. Match Group strongly supports the current language in the bill that subjects developers tonewst and ards to protect children. We donotseek to lessen or diminish the force of that language. We do believe that it only addresses part of the problem and that any loopholes will be exploited by bad actors seeking todoharm. App stores are not simply innate digital “infrastructure” with no input on the content of services. As the mobile app ecosystem operates today, Apple and Google have a direct monopoly of access and control over digital content, yettheydo not use their position as gatekeepers to protect those who use their services every day. In its ‘Mobile Ecosystems’ market study, the Competition and Markets Authority supports our view, firmly rejecting any suggestion that app stores are mere infrastructure: “Through the design choices that they make, through their individual practices, and through the rules and restrictions that they place on other market participants, Apple and Google are often in the position of acting in a quasi-regulatory capacity in relation to users’ security, privacy, and online safety. In many cases they opttomake decisions on behalf of consumers, with the stated aim of protecting them from bad actors or harmful consequences online. “However, it is not always clear if these numerous choices are made fully in the interests of consumers in all cases, for example where users’ security and privacy are the justification for decisions that also serve to harm competition or limit consumer choice.” Apple and Google control the means of distribution of online services for millions of apps and billions of users, yettheytakeno responsibility for the wellbeing of those users, despite profiting from commission charges between 15 and 30 per cent on certain in-app purchases. In the vast majority of instances, app stores have the data necessary to age-gate their platforms, preventing underage users from accessing apps with adult-only services and age-inappropriate content. The reality is that today the app stores choose to ignore age related dataanddo not share it with app developers, turning a blind eye to the matter. In fact, app stores directly facilitate usertouser experiences and additionally very much function as search engines. Apple’s developer in for mation itself talks about how to optimize for App Store search . There exists an entire industry that examines the factors thatgointoapp store search since the functioning search algorithm is not publicly known and is based on a variety of subjective factors dictated exclusively by the app store operators. Fur the rmore, we have repeatedly stated to both DCMS and Ofcom that despite numerous requests we have made to Apple, its app store still lists our portfolio brands’ services as being a vailable for individuals 17+ instead of 18+. The fact that Apple categorizes our brands as being for individuals 17 and older not only incorrectly describes our services, butalsohas a direct and meaningful impact on user behaviour, as

chunks.text: Apple, its app store still lists our portfolio brands’ services as being a vailable for individuals 17+ instead of 18+. The fact that Apple categorizes our brands as being for individuals 17 and older not only incorrectly describes our services, butalsohas a direct and meaningful impact on user behaviour, as Both Apple and Google recognize that they play a major role in facilitating usertouser content. At the distribution layer, both Apple and Google interact directly with consumers, collect substantial user datathattheyare able to monetize, and use subjective algorithms to influence and impact consumer behaviour. Recognizing their direct relationship with consumers, both companies have features allowing users to report inappropriate user-to-user content. As reported by Tech Crunch on October 4, 2021 (https://techcrunch. com/2021/10/06/with-return-of-report-a-problem-on-app-store-apple-invites-public-to-help-fight-fraud/): “Apple is making a change to its App Store by returning the “Report a Problem” link to App Store product pages across iOS 15, iPadOS 15 and macOS Monterey devices. The link offers an easy way for users to report issues they’re having with an app, including whether it contains offensive or illegal content, or whether it was a ttempting to scam consumers out of their money. Apple had removed the consumer-friendly “Report a Problem” button from the App Store years a go, possibly to its detriment. New reports have suggested that manyofthetop apps are scams costing consumers millions and Apple hasevenbeen questioned by Congress over the situation. The button’s return was spotted earlier this week , but Apple didn’t officially announce it until today.” Similarly, Google has a function specifically designed to allow reporting of age-inappropriate “family content”. See below: “How to report problems with family content We doourbest tomakesureany content with a family starismore carefully reviewed and is age appropriate. Ifyoufind inappropriate content, letusknow.” Match Group takes our responsibilities as an app developer incredibly seriously. We put trust and safety at the heart of everything we dotomeet our responsibility to users to foster positive interactions online. We consider that a fundamental priority. Match Group employs more than 450 employees across the portfolio who are focused on Trust & Safety. In 2021, the company invested $125 million to build new innovative technology and features tohelpmake dating safe for everyone. We have and will continue to invest and innovate in online safety features, but without the ‘bouncers of the digital world’ fulfilling their obligations, we will not achieve a successful holistic and sector-wide approach. To date, app store gatekeepers have declined to cooperate with us. I was therefore pleased to note the Committee’s interest regarding the absence of app stores from the scope of the Online Safety Bill, even while they hold the necessary userdataandage-assurance technologies to prevent children from accessing adult-only user services. The role of gatekeeper is crucial in ensuring that underage individuals do not access online services that are adult-only, yet companies such as Apple and Google have ignored our concerns, continuing to knowingly allow children to download adult apps. Addressing the distribution layer in the Bill is a clear way to elevate the effectiveness

chunks.text: is crucial in ensuring that underage individuals do not access online services that are adult-only, yet companies such as Apple and Google have ignored our concerns, continuing to knowingly allow children to download adult apps. Addressing the distribution layer in the Bill is a clear way to elevate the effectiveness The Government recently adopted an amendment (NC2) that specifically creates liability for executives atappsthatdo not take steps to protect children from inappropriate material. It took this action because it believed thatfartoo often online service operators were choosing not to act despite knowing that children were being exposed to inappropriate content. If user reporting was a sufficient solution, the Government would not have adopted the amendment. As explained in this letter, the same rules must therefore be a pplied to the app stores. We have engaged extensively with public consultations in support of the Online Safety Bill, including those from the Lords Communications and Digital Committee, the Digital, Culture, Media and Sport Select Committee, Ofcom, the CMA, and the Department of Digital, Culture, Media and Sport itself, highlighting the role of the distribution layer in the app ecosystem. We have been vocal about the need to include app stores with in the scope of the Bill, and we are therefore disappointed to see that DCMS appears to have fundamentally misunderstood the digital supply chain and how this impacts the en for cement of online safety regulation. Finally, I wanttotake this opportunity to repeat Match Group’s support for the Online Safety Billandthework of your Committee. We welcome this world-leading legislation and our responsibility to our users, and we will continue todoourpart in making the UK a safer place to be online. Should it be of assistance, I am at the disposal of your Committee to address questions as tohowtheapp ecosystem can work together to support the aims of this legislation. Sincerely, MarkBuse SVP, Head of Global Government Relations and Policy Match Group

It took this action because it believed thatfartoo often online service operators were choosing not to act despite knowing that children were being exposed to inappropriate content. We have engaged extensively with public consultations in support of the Online Safety Bill, including those from the Lords Communications and Digital Committee, the Digital, Culture, Media and Sport Select Committee, Ofcom, the CMA, and the Department of Digital, Culture, Media and Sport itself, highlighting the role of the distribution layer in the app ecosystem. We have been vocal about the need to include app stores with in the scope of the Bill, and we are therefore disappointed to see that DCMS appears to have fundamentally misunderstood the digital supply chain and how this impacts the en for cement of online safety regulation. Finally, I wanttotake this opportunity to repeat Match Group’s support for the Online Safety Billandthework of your Committee. We welcome this world-leading legislation and our responsibility to our users, and we will continue todoourpart in making the UK a safer place to be online. Should it be of assistance, I am at the disposal of your Committee to address questions as tohowtheapp ecosystem can work together to support the aims of this legislation. Sincerely, MarkBuse SVP, Head of Global Government Relations and Policy Match Group
